paragraphs: Amber Jewel is an Australian-bred Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) that began as a chance seedling found in 1978 in the garden of R. A. Yates at Capel River, Western Australia. It was originally named Teak Gold, patented under that name, then renamed Amber Jewel for market. The fruit is large and conical with red skin over a cream to yellow background.

Its standout feature is amber to golden-yellow flesh that is very sweet, meaty, and crisp with low acidity. Amber Jewel matures late, about a month after Santa Rosa, and is partially self-fertile, pairing well with pollinators such as Santa Rosa or Laroda. It also has notably good storage and shelf life.

claims.text: Australian-bred Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) that began as a chance seedling found in 1978 by R. A. Yates at Capel River, Western Australia, originally named Teak Gold
Large conical fruit with red skin over a cream to yellow background
Amber to golden-yellow flesh that is very sweet, meaty, and crisp with low acidity
Matures late, about a month after Santa Rosa, and is partially self-fertile with pollinators such as Santa Rosa or Laroda
Notably good storage and shelf life

sources.name: US Plant Patent PP10277: Plum tree Teak Gold

paragraphs: Angelina is the late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) widely marketed as Angeleno or Angelino. It was released in the United States in 1995 by Sunworld International and has become one of the dominant very late plums in commercial markets worldwide.

The skin colours from green to a dark, almost black purple when ripe, over firm pale-yellow to amber flesh. The flavour is sweet and well balanced, with dense, crunchy flesh rather than soft and juicy. It ripens very late and stores and ships exceptionally well, keeping firm in the bowl for a long time.

claims.text: Late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) marketed as Angeleno or Angelino, released in the United States in 1995 by Sunworld International
Skin ripens from green to dark, almost black purple, over firm pale-yellow to amber flesh
Sweet, well-balanced flavour with dense, firm, crunchy flesh rather than soft and juicy
Ripens very late and stores and ships exceptionally well

sources.name: Tough Kraut: Prunus salicina 'Angeleno' care guide

paragraphs: Angelina Burdett is a heritage English dessert plum (Prunus domestica), raised by Mr Dowling of Woolston, Southampton, in about 1850 and first described in 1853. It is a completely separate variety from the modern Angeleno (Angelina) plum.

The fruit is medium sized and round-oval with purple to blue-black skin carrying a heavy bloom, over firm, juicy, yellowish-green flesh with good flavour. It is grown mainly as a dessert plum, has a semi-freestone stone, hangs and keeps well after picking, and ripens from late August into early September.

claims.text: Heritage English dessert plum (Prunus domestica) raised by Mr Dowling of Woolston, Southampton, about 1850 and first described in 1853
Medium round-oval fruit with purple to blue-black bloomed skin over firm, juicy, yellowish-green flesh
Grown mainly as a dessert plum
Semi-freestone, hangs and keeps well after picking, ripening late August into early September

paragraphs: Angelino (Angeleno) is a late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released in the USA in 1995 by Sun World International. The skin is dark red, almost black, over pale yellow flesh with a small stone.

Its flesh is dense, firm and crunchy with a sweet, well-balanced flavour built up over a long hang time on the tree. Because it stores and ships unusually well, it dominates the late-season plum window, reaching shops as other varieties finish.

claims.text: Late-season Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released in the USA in 1995 by Sun World International
Dark red, almost black skin over pale yellow flesh with a small stone; dense, firm, crunchy, sweet
Stores and ships well, dominating the late-season plum window

paragraphs: Black Amber is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) raised in Fresno, California by USDA breeder J.H. Weinberger from a Friar by Queen Rosa cross, released in 1980. It carries large, round fruit with dark purple to near-black skin over firm amber flesh.

The flavour is sweet and well balanced, and the fruit holds its firmness well, which made it a long-standing commercial shipping plum. It ripens mid season and is partially self-fertile, cropping more heavily with a compatible Japanese plum nearby. It resembles its parent Friar in look.

claims.text: Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) raised in Fresno, California by USDA breeder J.H. Weinberger from a Friar by Queen Rosa cross, released 1980
Dark purple to near-black skin over firm amber flesh, sweet, mid season
Partially self-fertile, crops more heavily with a pollenizer; resembles Friar

paragraphs: Black Diamond is a Sun World trademarked series of black-skinned, red-fleshed Japanese plums (Prunus salicina), not a single seedling. The skin is deep purple to almost black and the flesh is red to scarlet.

The eating character is crisp and firm with a sweet, juicy flavour carrying a hint of tartness, and the dark skin is mild rather than bitter. In Australia it crops over summer (roughly November to January) and, like most Japanese plums, sets best with a cross-pollinator such as Santa Rosa.

claims.text: A Sun World trademarked series of black-skinned, red-fleshed Japanese plums
Deep purple to near-black skin, red to scarlet flesh, crisp, sweet with a hint of tartness, skin not bitter
Australian summer cropping (about November to January); benefits from a cross-pollinator

sources.name: Fruitnet (Eurofruit): Sun World Black Diamond plum series
The Yummy Fruit Co: Black Diamond

paragraphs: The Burdekin plum is not a true plum but the fruit of Pleiogynium timoriense, a rainforest tree in the Anacardiaceae family (the same family as cashew and mango), not the genus Prunus. It is native to north-eastern Queensland and extends north of Australia through Malesia to the Pacific, and is also called sweet plum or tulip plum.

The fruit is round and squat, around 3 to 4 cm across, ripening to a deep purple or near black, with a notably large stone that leaves little flesh. Picked fruit is hard and tart, so it is traditionally left to soften and sweeten after harvest before being eaten raw or made into jams. It was an important bush food for Aboriginal peoples.

claims.text: Fruit of Pleiogynium timoriense in the Anacardiaceae (cashew and mango) family, native to north-eastern Queensland, not a true Prunus plum
Round, squat fruit about 3 to 4 cm across, ripening deep purple to black with a large stone
Hard and astringent when picked, softening and sweetening after harvest before eating or making into jam

sources.name: Australian Native Plants Society: Pleiogynium timoriense

paragraphs: Coe's Golden Drop is a heritage English European plum (Prunus domestica), raised by Jervaise Coe in Suffolk at the end of the 18th century, said to be a cross between Green Gage and White Magnum Bonum. It is one of the historic European dessert plums and considered among the best of the late-season varieties.

The fruit has golden yellow skin, often flecked with reddish brown and carrying a cinnamon-like bloom, over tender, juicy golden flesh. The flavour is rich and sweet with a distinctive apricot character, balanced by some acidity from its Magnum parent. It is a large freestone plum that ripens late, around mid-September, and needs a pollinator such as Green Gage or D'Agen to set fruit.

claims.text: Heritage English European plum (Prunus domestica) raised by Jervaise Coe in Suffolk at the end of the 18th century, reputedly Green Gage crossed with White Magnum Bonum
A historic European dessert plum, regarded as one of the best late-season varieties
Golden yellow skin with reddish brown flecks and a cinnamon bloom over tender golden flesh
Rich, sweet flavour with a distinctive apricot character, balanced by some acidity
Large freestone fruit that ripens late, around mid-September
Needs a pollinator such as Green Gage or D'Agen to set fruit

sources.name: Gardening Know How: Coe's Golden Drop Gage

paragraphs: The D'Agen prune (Prune d'Agen, also called Petite d'Agen, Ente or the California French prune) is a European plum (Prunus domestica) originating in the Agen region of southwestern France, the classic plum grown for drying into prunes. In California it is known simply as the French prune, and what Europeans call d'Agen is the same variety.

It is a small to medium purple to blue-skinned plum with firm, sweet, yellow-green flesh, prized for its dense flesh and high sugar that concentrate beautifully when dried. The variety was introduced to California by Louis Pellier in the 1850s, founding the Californian prune industry. It is best known for drying and prune making rather than fresh dessert use.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica) from the Agen region of southwestern France, the classic drying/prune plum
Also called Petite d'Agen, Ente, and the California French prune; the French prune in California equals the d'Agen of Europe
Small to medium plum with purple to blue skin and firm, sweet yellow-green flesh
Grown chiefly for drying into prunes, with flesh and sugar that concentrate well when dried
Introduced to California by Louis Pellier in the 1850s, founding the Californian prune industry

sources.name: Taste of France: What exactly is an Agen prune?

paragraphs: The Damson is a small European plum (Prunus domestica subsp. insititia) whose name derives from the Latin for plum of Damascus. It is a traditional cooking plum long grown in Britain, with skin ranging from dark blue through indigo to near black over yellow-green flesh, and a typically ovoid shape pointed at one end.

Damsons are distinctive for being both high in sugar and highly astringent, giving a rich flavour that is usually considered too tart for fresh eating. Most are clingstone, with the flesh adhering to the stone. They are grown mainly for cooking and preserving, going into jam, fruit preserves, and damson gin, and ripen in late summer to autumn.

claims.text: Small European plum, Prunus domestica subsp. insititia, named from the Latin for plum of Damascus
Dark blue to near-black skin over yellow-green flesh, ovoid and pointed at one end
Both high in sugar and highly astringent, with a rich tart flavour usually too tart for fresh eating
Most are clingstone, with flesh adhering to the stone
Grown mainly for cooking and preserving, including jam and damson gin

paragraphs: Donsworth is a Japanese blood plum (Prunus salicina) long grown in Australia. It appears in the Heritage and Rare Fruits Network listing of Japanese plums, with grower records across several Australian sites, marking it as an established, recognised cultivar rather than a recent release.
It is an early season, large clingstone plum with dark pink to red skin and juicy, light red flesh. A vigorous, heavy-bearing tree, it suits fresh eating as well as jams and preserves.

claims.text: Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) recognised as an established cultivar grown in Australia
Early season, large clingstone fruit with dark pink to red skin and juicy light red flesh
Vigorous, prolific bearer suited to fresh eating and to jams and preserves

sources.name: Heritage and Rare Fruits Network: Plums
Heritage Fruit Trees: Donsworth Japanese Blood Plum

paragraphs: Elephant Heart is a large Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) raised by Luther Burbank in California in the early 1920s and released after his death. The fruit is big and heart-shaped with thick bronze-green skin that turns reddish-purple when fully ripe.

It is best known for its juicy, blood-red, freestone flesh and rich, sweet, distinctive flavour, which is why it is grouped among the blood plums. Elephant Heart ripens very late in the season, one of the last plums to mature, and crops better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa. It is excellent fresh and also used for canning and freezing.

claims.text: Large Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) raised by Luther Burbank in California in the early 1920s and released after his death
Large heart-shaped fruit with thick bronze-green skin turning reddish-purple when ripe
Juicy blood-red freestone flesh with a rich sweet distinctive flavour
Ripens very late, among the last plums to mature, and crops better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa

sources.name: Trees of Antiquity: Elephant Heart Plum

paragraphs: Flavour Supreme (sold as Flavor Supreme) is a pluot, an interspecific plum and apricot hybrid developed by Floyd Zaiger of Zaiger Genetics in California. Dave Wilson Nursery, the introducer of Zaiger's interspecific fruit, records it as the first pluot Zaiger released, in 1988.

The fruit has mottled green skin with red speckling, an unassuming look that hides intensely sweet, juicy, red (Bordeaux) flesh. It is one of the earlier pluots to ripen and is not self-fertile, so it needs a polleniser such as another pluot or a Japanese plum nearby to set a crop.

claims.text: A pluot (interspecific plum and apricot hybrid) developed by Floyd Zaiger of Zaiger Genetics
Introduced by Dave Wilson Nursery as the first pluot Zaiger released, in 1988
Mottled green skin with red speckling over juicy red (Bordeaux) flesh
Intensely sweet, rich, juicy flesh
Not self-fertile, requires a polleniser such as another pluot variety

sources.name: Dave Wilson Nursery: Flavor Supreme Pluot

paragraphs: The Governor's plum is not a true plum but Flacourtia indica, a thorny shrub or small tree of the willow family (Salicaceae). It also goes by ramontchi, batoko plum, Madagascar plum and Indian plum. It is native to much of Africa, Madagascar and tropical to temperate parts of Asia, and bears spiny trunk and branches.

The small fruit is about an inch (2.5 cm) across and ripens from red to purple, with yellow or white flesh that is sweet with an acidic tang and several seeds in layered carpels. It is eaten raw or made into jam, jelly and wine, and various plant parts are used in traditional medicine.

claims.text: Governor's plum is Flacourtia indica, a thorny shrub or tree of the willow family (Salicaceae), not a true plum
Also called ramontchi, batoko plum, Madagascar plum and Indian plum
Native to much of Africa, Madagascar and tropical to temperate Asia
Fruit about an inch across, red ripening to purple, sweet with an acidic tang and several seeds
Eaten raw or made into jam, jelly and wine

paragraphs: Green Gage is a European plum (Prunus domestica), the type known in France as Reine Claude after Queen Claude, wife of Francis I. The English name honours the Gage family, credited with introducing it to England in the early 18th century. The fruit is small and round-oval with green to yellowish-green skin and smooth pale green flesh.

Gages are prized as one of the finest dessert plums, with a rich, sweet, confectionery flavour, though they are also eaten unripe when they are crisp and pleasantly tart. Green Gage ripens in late summer and is not self-fertile, so it needs a compatible plum, gage, or damson nearby to pollinate it. It is eaten fresh and also stewed or made into jam.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica), known in France as Reine Claude after Queen Claude, with the English name honouring the Gage family
Small round-oval fruit with green to yellowish skin and smooth pale green flesh
Among the finest dessert plums, with a rich sweet confectionery flavour, also eaten unripe when tart
Not self-fertile, needing another plum, gage, or damson as a pollinator
Eaten fresh and also stewed or made into jam

paragraphs: Gulf Beauty (Gulfbeauty) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released by the University of Florida in 1998, part of the Gulf series bred by Wayne Sherman for mild-winter, subtropical regions with high disease pressure. All UF plum cultivars carry the Gulf prefix.

It is one of the earliest ripening plums in the series, with dark reddish purple skin over yellow flesh tinged green, sweet and sub-acid when ripe (the skin itself is sour). It is noted as very resistant to bacterial canker and moderately resistant to plum leaf scald, and benefits from a Gulf series pollinator.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) released by the University of Florida in 1998, part of the Gulf series bred by Wayne Sherman
Bred for mild-winter, subtropical regions; low-chill
One of the earliest ripening plums of the series
Dark reddish purple skin over yellow flesh with a green hue
Sweet and sub-acid flesh, firm when ripe, with a sour skin
Very resistant to bacterial canker and moderately resistant to plum leaf scald

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Gulf Blaze (Gulfblaze) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred and patented by the University of Florida as part of the Gulf series, developed by Wayne Sherman for warm, subtropical climates with high disease pressure.

The fruit has dark red to purple skin over orange flesh that is sweet and sub-acid. It ripens in the middle of the Florida plum season, roughly 8 to 14 days after Gulf Beauty. The flesh is semi-freestone, weakly attached to the pit when ripe, and the fruit is firm and round.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred and patented by the University of Florida Gulf series, developed by Wayne Sherman
Bred for warm, subtropical climates; low-chill
Dark red to purple skin over orange flesh
Flesh is sweet and sub-acid
Ripens mid-season, about 8 to 14 days after Gulf Beauty
Semi-freestone, with flesh weakly attached to the pit when ripe

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Gulf Gold (Gulfgold) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida stone fruit breeding program, part of the Gulf series bred for mild-winter, subtropical climates. Unlike the patented members of the series, it is not patented.

It is a yellow-skinned plum, often with a red blush, over yellow flesh that turns semi-soft when ripe, and is regarded as the sweetest of the Gulf series. It ripens late in the series, from late May into mid-June. A drawback is its susceptibility to plum leaf scald, which generally limits tree life in Florida.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida Gulf series; not patented
Bred for mild-winter, subtropical climates; low-chill
Yellow skin with a red blush over yellow, semi-soft flesh
Sweetest of the Gulf series
Ripens late in the series, late May to mid-June
Susceptible to plum leaf scald, which generally limits tree life in Florida

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: Gulf Ruby (Gulfruby) is a low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida stone fruit breeding program led by Wayne Sherman, part of the Gulf series bred for warm, subtropical climates with high disease pressure. It was selected and propagated in the early 1980s and, unlike most of the series, is not patented and may be freely propagated.

The fruit has red to purple skin over sweet yellow flesh with a greenish tinge. It ripens early in the Florida plum season, roughly 7 to 10 days before Gulf Blaze. A noted quirk is that Gulf Ruby has more trouble with bacterial canker than other low-chill cultivars, which can shorten tree life.

claims.text: Low-chill Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) from the University of Florida Gulf series, bred by Wayne Sherman; selected/propagated in the early 1980s and not patented
Low-chill cultivar bred for warm, subtropical climates
Red to purple skin over sweet yellow flesh with a greenish tinge
Ripens early in the Florida plum season, about 7 to 10 days before Gulf Blaze
More prone to bacterial canker than other low-chill cultivars

sources.name: UF/IFAS EDIS HS250: Growing Plums in Florida
UF/IFAS News: Four New Plum Varieties Developed By UF Researcher

paragraphs: The hog plum is not a true plum but most commonly refers to Spondias mombin (also called yellow mombin), a tree of the cashew and mango family (Anacardiaceae). It is native to the tropical Americas and the West Indies, and was carried by the Portuguese to Asia and Africa, where it has naturalised. True plums sit in a different family (Rosaceae).

The ovoid fruit is nearly 4 cm long and ripens yellow, with a leathery skin over a thin layer of sharp, somewhat acid pulp surrounding a single spiny kernel. It is eaten fresh or made into juice, jellies and sherbets, and the green fruit features in dishes such as Thai som tam and Nepali pickles.

claims.text: Hog plum most commonly refers to Spondias mombin (yellow mombin), in the cashew/mango family (Anacardiaceae), not a true plum
Native to the tropical Americas and the West Indies, later naturalised in Asia and Africa
Ovoid yellow fruit nearly 4 cm long with leathery skin and a single spiny kernel
Sharp, somewhat acid pulp eaten fresh or made into juice, jellies and sherbets

sources.name: Plants For A Future: Spondias mombin

paragraphs: The Illawarra plum is not a true plum but comes from Podocarpus elatus, a rainforest conifer in the Podocarpaceae family (not the genus Prunus). Also called plum pine or brown pine, it is native to the east coast of Australia in New South Wales and Queensland. Because it is a conifer, the seed is not enclosed in a fruit.

What people eat is a swollen fleshy stem (receptacle) that the single seed sits on, attached to the branch, ripening to a dark purple-black. It has a grape-like texture and a sweetish, mildly resinous pine flavour that is enhanced by cooking, and is used in jams, sauces, and bush-food dishes. The hard seed itself is discarded.

claims.text: Comes from Podocarpus elatus, a conifer in the Podocarpaceae family native to eastern Australia, not a true Prunus plum
The edible part is a swollen fleshy receptacle that the single seed sits on, not a true fruit (conifer seeds are not surrounded by a fruit)
Dark purple-black, grape-like texture with a sweetish mild pine flavour enhanced by cooking

sources.name: Australian National Botanic Gardens: Podocarpus elatus

paragraphs: The jambolan is not a true plum but the fruit of Syzygium cumini, an evergreen tree of the myrtle family (Myrtaceae), the same family as eucalyptus and guava. It is native to India, Burma (Myanmar), Sri Lanka and the Andaman Islands, and has naturalised across southern Asia and the tropics.

The clustered fruit ripens from green through light magenta to dark purple or nearly black, with juicy flesh that is often astringent and ranges from acid to fairly sweet. It is eaten fresh and turned into tarts, jam, jelly, syrup, wine and vinegar. The juice resembles grape juice and the fruit famously stains the mouth.

claims.text: Fruit of Syzygium cumini, an evergreen tree of the myrtle family (Myrtaceae), not a true plum
Native to India, Burma, Sri Lanka and the Andaman Islands
Fruit ripens from green through light magenta to dark purple or nearly black
Astringent, acid-to-sweet juicy flesh used fresh and for jam, wine and vinegar
Fruit stains the mouth purple

sources.name: Purdue NewCROP, Morton 'Fruits of Warm Climates': Jambolan (Internet Archive)

paragraphs: The Java plum is not a true plum (genus Prunus) but Syzygium cumini, an evergreen tropical tree of the myrtle family (Myrtaceae), kin to eucalyptus and guava. It is native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia, where it is also known as jambolan, jamun and black plum, and it has naturalised widely in warm climates.

The oblong fruit colours from green to pink to deep purple-black as it matures. The flesh is juicy with a sweet, tart and astringent taste that tends to dye the tongue purple. It is eaten fresh and processed into juice, wine, vinegar and syrups. The species is considered invasive in parts of Florida, Hawaii and South Africa.

claims.text: Java plum is Syzygium cumini, a myrtle-family (Myrtaceae) tree, not a true Prunus plum
Native to the Indian subcontinent and Southeast Asia
Fruit colours from green to pink to deep purple-black and dyes the tongue purple
Eaten fresh and made into juice, wine, vinegar and syrups
Considered invasive in parts of Florida, Hawaii and South Africa

sources.name: Purdue NewCROP, Morton 'Fruits of Warm Climates': Jambolan (Internet Archive)

paragraphs: Jefferson is an old gage type European plum (Prunus domestica) raised from seed by Judge Buel of Albany, New York, around 1825, and named in honour of US President Thomas Jefferson. Its parentage is uncertain but it is thought to be a seedling of Coe's Golden Drop, which it resembles. It reached England in the 1840s.

The fruit is large and oval with golden yellow skin, often a pinkish red cheek and light bloom. The deep yellow flesh is juicy with a rich, sweet gage flavour balanced by some sharpness, making it a prized dessert plum. It ripens late in the season and the fruit does not all ripen at once.

claims.text: Raised by Judge Buel in Albany, New York, around 1825, named after President Jefferson
Likely a seedling of Coe's Golden Drop, which it resembles
Large golden yellow fruit with rich sweet gage flavour, a dessert plum

paragraphs: The Kaffir plum is not a true plum but Harpephyllum caffrum, an evergreen tree of the cashew and mango family (Anacardiaceae). The older common name is now widely replaced by wild plum (or wildepruim in Afrikaans). The genus name refers to its sickle shaped leaflets. It is native to southern Africa, ranging from the Eastern Cape north through KwaZulu-Natal and into Zimbabwe.

The small fruit is a drupe that ripens from green to bright red in autumn and contains a single seed. It is distinctly tart and sour, which suits it better to preserves than to fresh eating. People use it for jams, jellies and even a rose wine, while the bark is used traditionally for skin washes.

claims.text: Harpephyllum caffrum, a tree of the cashew/mango family (Anacardiaceae), not a true plum
Older name Kaffir plum now widely replaced by wild plum (wildepruim)
Native to southern Africa, from the Eastern Cape north through KwaZulu-Natal into Zimbabwe
Small fruit ripens green to bright red with a single seed, tart and sour
Used for jams, jellies and rose wine; bark used traditionally for skin washes

paragraphs: The Kakadu plum is not a true plum but the fruit of Terminalia ferdinandiana, a small tree in the Combretaceae family (not the genus Prunus). It grows wild across the tropical woodlands of northern Australia, from the Kimberley to Arnhem Land, and is also known as gubinge or billygoat plum. Aboriginal peoples have eaten it and used the bark and fruit medicinally for thousands of years.

The fruit is small, yellow-green, roughly almond sized, with a single stone. It is prized today as a native superfood because it is among the richest natural sources of vitamin C known, far exceeding common fruits. Almost all supply is still wild harvested by hand, and the fruit is processed into powders, jams, and skincare rather than eaten fresh in quantity.

claims.text: Fruit of Terminalia ferdinandiana (Combretaceae), native to northern Australia, not a true Prunus plum
Small yellow-green, almond-sized fruit with a single stone
Among the richest natural sources of vitamin C known, with levels far exceeding common fruits
Largely wild harvested by Aboriginal communities and used as bush food and medicine

sources.name: Nutritional and Functional Properties of Terminalia ferdinandiana Fruits (PMC11431513)
AgriFutures Australia: Australian Bush Plum (Terminalia ferdinandiana) collection

paragraphs: Mariposa is a Japanese-type blood plum that originated as a seedling at Pasadena, California, around 1923. It is sometimes sold as Improved Satsuma.

The fruit has deep red, juicy flesh and a rich, sweet, aromatic flavour with a tart skin, and the stone separates almost cleanly (nearly freestone). It ripens mid season (late summer in Australia). Mariposa crops best with a compatible pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Satsuma.

claims.text: Japanese-type blood plum originating as a seedling at Pasadena, California, around 1923
A Japanese blood plum, also called Improved Satsuma
Deep red juicy flesh, rich sweet aromatic flavour, nearly freestone
Ripens mid season
Best cropped with a compatible pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Satsuma

paragraphs: Mirabelle is a group of small European plums (Prunus domestica subsp. syriaca) most closely associated with the Lorraine region of France, where it has been grown for centuries. The two best known cultivars are Mirabelle de Nancy and Mirabelle de Metz.

The fruit is small and round to oval with golden yellow skin, often flecked or dappled with red, and sweet, aromatic yellow flesh around a freestone pit. Its high sugar content suits it to jam and to distillation into eau de vie, a clear fruit brandy, as well as eating fresh and baking.

claims.text: A small plum group of the Prunus domestica subsp. syriaca, associated with Lorraine, France
Small golden yellow freestone fruit with sweet aromatic flesh
Widely used for jam and for distillation into eau de vie

sources.name: Specialty Produce: Mirabelle de Metz Plums

paragraphs: Narrabeen is an Australian Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) that arose as a chance seedling in the Narrabeen area near Sydney. It produces large, round fruit with red skin over a yellow background.

The flesh is yellow to cream, sweet, firm, and juicy, and the stone separates cleanly, making it a freestone variety. Narrabeen ripens mid season (around January to February in Australia) and crops best with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa, Satsuma, or Mariposa. It is good eaten fresh and also stewed, bottled, or made into jam.

claims.text: Australian Japanese-type plum (Prunus salicina) that arose as a chance seedling in the Narrabeen area near Sydney
Large round fruit with red skin over a yellow background
Yellow to cream flesh that is sweet, firm, and juicy, and freestone
Ripens mid season and crops best with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa, Satsuma, or Mariposa
Good fresh and also stewed, bottled, or made into jam

sources.name: Heritage Fruit Trees: Narrabeen Japanese Plum

paragraphs: The Natal plum is not a true plum but the fruit of Carissa macrocarpa, a spiny evergreen shrub in the dogbane family (Apocynaceae), not the genus Prunus. It is native to the coastal regions of southern Africa, where it is also known as the large num-num, and is widely grown as an ornamental and security hedge thanks to its forked spines and fragrant white flowers.

The plant contains a milky latex, and only the ripe fruit is eaten; other parts are considered toxic. The plump, oval berries ripen to a deep crimson and have a tangy-sweet, raspberry-cranberry flavour. They are eaten fresh or cooked into jams, jellies, sauces, and pies.

claims.text: Fruit of Carissa macrocarpa in the Apocynaceae (dogbane) family, native to coastal southern Africa, not a true Prunus plum
A spiny evergreen shrub containing milky latex, where only the ripe fruit is eaten and other parts are regarded as toxic
Crimson, plump oval berries with a tangy-sweet raspberry-cranberry flavour, eaten fresh or made into jams and jellies

sources.name: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox: Carissa macrocarpa (Natal Plum)

paragraphs: President is a European plum (Prunus domestica) raised by the Rivers Nursery at Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire, England, and introduced around 1901. It was valued as one of the latest plums to ripen, providing fresh fruit when most other plums had finished.

The fruit is very large and oval with dark blue to purple-black skin and greenish-yellow flesh, and it suits both eating fresh and culinary use such as jam and preserves. It ripens late, in mid September in the UK. President is not self-fertile, so it needs another plum variety nearby for pollination.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica) raised by the Rivers Nursery, Sawbridgeworth, Hertfordshire, introduced around 1901
Very large oval fruit with dark blue to purple-black skin and greenish-yellow flesh
Used both as a dessert plum and for culinary purposes such as preserves
A very late-ripening plum, ripening in mid September in the UK
Not self-fertile, requires another plum variety nearby for pollination

paragraphs: Primetime is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) that originated in California. Its US plant patent records it as believed to be an F1 hybrid of 'Challenger' and 'Showtime', discovered by Eric Wuhl in a Fresno orchard and asexually propagated in 1987.

The fruit is very large and uniform, with skin that turns from reddish to a very dark, almost eggplant colour when fully ripe, over firm, crisp yellow flesh shot through with red and pink veining. Santa Rosa is a suggested pollinator, and in Australia it ripens early to mid season.

claims.text: Believed to be an F1 hybrid of 'Challenger' and 'Showtime', discovered in Fresno, California and asexually propagated in 1987
Very large, uniform fruit
Skin ripens from reddish to a very dark, almost eggplant colour over firm, crisp yellow flesh with red and pink veining
Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) with Santa Rosa as a suggested pollinator

sources.name: US Plant Patent USPP9022P: Primetime plum tree

paragraphs: Purple Gage is the English name for Reine Claude Violette, a traditional French gage type plum (Prunus domestica) first recorded in France around 1780. It belongs to the Reine Claude group of plums, which are prized as dessert fruit.

Unlike the green skinned gages, its medium, round fruit has purple to violet skin dusted with a bloom and dotted with yellow specks. The greenish amber flesh is firm, very juicy and sugary with a rich, sweet gage flavour. It ripens in late summer and is eaten fresh as a dessert plum.

claims.text: English name for Reine Claude Violette, a French gage first recorded around 1780
Purple to violet skin with yellow dots, unlike green skinned gages
Juicy, sugary greenish amber flesh with rich sweet gage flavour, a dessert plum

sources.name: National Fruit Collection: Reine Claude Violette

paragraphs: Robe de Sergeant is an old French prune plum (Prunus domestica) that originated in France and was taken to California in the 1880s by the nurseryman Felix Gillet. Once planted in Californian prune districts, it fell out of commercial use because it yielded less than the Improved French prune.

The fruit is medium and roundish oval with deep purple to almost black skin under a thick grey bloom, and greenish yellow freestone flesh. It is richly flavoured, sweet and very juicy, and is used for drying into prunes, eating fresh, and for jam and preserves.

claims.text: Old French prune plum taken to California in the 1880s by Felix Gillet
Declined commercially due to lower yield than the Improved French prune
Deep purple to blackish freestone fruit, used for drying into prunes, fresh and jam

sources.name: Slow Food Foundation, Ark of Taste: Robe de Sergeant Plum
Heritage Fruit Trees: Robe de Sergeant Prune

paragraphs: Ruby Blood is a Japanese-type blood plum (Prunus salicina) grown widely in Australia. The fruit is medium to large and round to heart-shaped, with dark red mottled skin over deep red, juicy flesh, which is what marks it out as a blood plum.

The flesh is sweet and the stone separates cleanly, making it a freestone variety. Ruby Blood ripens late in the season (late summer into autumn in Australia) and needs a cross-pollinator such as Narrabeen, Mariposa, or Satsuma. It is used fresh and also for preserves and jam.

claims.text: Japanese-type blood plum (Prunus salicina) grown widely in Australia
Medium to large round to heart-shaped fruit with dark red mottled skin over deep red juicy flesh
Sweet flesh, freestone with the stone separating cleanly
Ripens late in the season and needs a cross-pollinator such as Narrabeen, Mariposa, or Satsuma
Used fresh and also for preserves and jam

sources.name: Blerick Tree Farm: Plum Ruby Blood
Heritage Fruit Trees: Ruby Japanese Blood Plum

paragraphs: Santa Rosa is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred by Luther Burbank and introduced in 1906 from his nursery at Santa Rosa, California. It became Burbank's most celebrated plum and was once the dominant commercial plum in California.

The fruit has purple-red skin over amber to yellow flesh, with a sweet, slightly tart flavour. It ripens mid season. Santa Rosa is self-fertile and is widely used as a pollinator for other Japanese plums, though crops improve when grown near a second variety.

claims.text: Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) bred by Luther Burbank, introduced 1906 from Santa Rosa, California
Purple-red skin over yellow to amber flesh, sweet and slightly tart
Ripens mid season
Self-fertile and a widely used pollinator for Japanese plums

sources.name: Trees of Antiquity: Santa Rosa Plum

paragraphs: Satsuma is a Japanese plum (Prunus salicina) introduced to the United States by Luther Burbank in the late 1800s and named after the Satsuma province of Japan. It was an early and influential blood plum in commercial cultivation.

It is known as a blood plum for its dark red skin and deep red flesh, with sweet, juicy fruit. It ripens mid season. Satsuma is at most partially self-fertile and crops far better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Shiro.

claims.text: Japanese plum introduced to the US by Luther Burbank in the late 1800s, named after the Satsuma province of Japan
A blood plum with dark red skin and deep red flesh
Sweet, juicy fruit
Ripens mid season
At most partially self-fertile, crops better with a pollinator such as Santa Rosa or Shiro

sources.name: Gardening Know How: How To Grow Satsuma Plums

paragraphs: Sloe is the fruit of blackthorn (Prunus spinosa), a thorny wild shrub native to Europe and West Asia, not a cultivated dessert plum. Its dense, spiny growth has long been used to make stockproof hedges in Britain and northern Europe.

The small drupe, about 10 to 12 millimetres across, is black with a blue purple bloom and ripens in autumn. The thin flesh is intensely astringent when raw and is traditionally gathered after the first frosts, which softens it. Sloes are used to make sloe gin, where they are steeped with gin and sugar, and in jams, jellies and preserves rather than eaten fresh.

claims.text: Fruit of blackthorn (Prunus spinosa), a wild thorny shrub used for hedging
Small black fruit with blue purple bloom, about 10 to 12 mm across, ripening in autumn
Strongly astringent raw, traditionally picked after frost and used for sloe gin and preserves

sources.name: Eatweeds: The Traditional and Modern Uses of Sloe (Blackthorn)

paragraphs: Spring Satin is a plumcot, an interspecific cross of plum and apricot, and was the first plumcot cultivar released by a public breeding program. It was developed by William (Dick) Okie at the USDA ARS Southeastern Fruit and Tree Nut Research Laboratory in Byron, Georgia, and bred specifically for the humid, medium to high chill conditions of the southeastern United States, where most California plumcots were poorly adapted.

The fruit is reddish black to purple skinned with flesh that is yellow when immature, turning yellowish red as it ripens, and has a complex, plum like flavour. It ripens early in the stone fruit season. A useful trait is its tolerance to major plum diseases including bacterial spot, bacterial canker, and plum leaf scald. It looks much like a Japanese plum and sets best when cross pollinated with another Japanese plum.

claims.text: Spring Satin is an interspecific plum x apricot plumcot and was the first plumcot cultivar released by a public breeding program.
It was developed by William (Dick) Okie at the USDA ARS laboratory in Byron, Georgia, for the humid medium to high chill southeastern United States.
The fruit has reddish black skin and flesh that is yellow when immature, changing to yellowish red when mature, with a complex plum like flavour.
It is an early ripening variety in the stone fruit season.
It is tolerant to major plum diseases including bacterial spot, bacterial canker, and plum leaf scald.
It resembles a Japanese plum and sets fruit best when cross pollinated with another Japanese plum.

sources.name: USDA ARS publication: Spring Satin plumcot (Okie)
USDA ARS news: New Plum-Apricot Cross Ideal for Southern Growers

paragraphs: The Sugar prune is a European plum (Prunus domestica) introduced by Luther Burbank in 1899, raised as a seedling of the French (Agen) prune. It quickly took on commercial importance in the Californian prune districts in the early 1900s.

The fruit is medium to large, larger than the French prune, oval and slightly flattened, with dark reddish purple skin under a thick white bloom. It is freestone, with golden yellow flesh that is juicy, tender and sweet with high sugar content. Despite its name, it tended to dry into a coarse, stringy product, so it was shipped extensively as a fresh plum and used for canning rather than as a top-quality dried prune.

claims.text: European plum (Prunus domestica) introduced by Luther Burbank in 1899 as a seedling of the French (Agen) prune
Took on commercial importance in Californian prune districts from 1903
Medium to large, oval and slightly flattened, with dark reddish purple skin and a thick white bloom; freestone
Golden yellow flesh that is juicy, tender and sweet with high sugar content
Intended as a prune but dries coarse and stringy, so shipped mainly as a fresh plum and used for canning

sources.name: HortScience (ASHS): Luther Burbank's Plums, Vol. 50 Issue 2 (2015)

paragraphs: Sultan Blood is a Japanese blood plum (Prunus salicina). The Heritage and Rare Fruits Network records it as a cross between Satsuma and Wickson, producing a large, oval, sweet fruit with dark crimson colouring.

As a blood plum it carries the deep red flesh typical of the group. It is a late ripening variety, with the same archive noting a February to March fruiting window. Like most Japanese plums it generally benefits from a compatible pollinator nearby.

claims.text: Japanese blood plum (Prunus salicina) recorded as a cross between Satsuma and Wickson
Large, oval, sweet fruit with dark crimson colouring
Late ripening, fruiting around February to March
Prunus salicina is mostly self-incompatible and generally needs cross pollination

sources.name: Heritage and Rare Fruits Network: Plums

paragraphs: Victoria is the famous English plum (Prunus domestica), found as a seedling at Alderton, England, and introduced by the nurseryman Denyer in the 1840s as Denyer's Victoria. It went on to become the most popular plum variety in the UK.

The fruit has pale red, lightly speckled skin over golden-yellow flesh, and it is unusually versatile, good both fresh and cooked, where it makes excellent jam and pie fillings. Victoria is reliably self-fertile, so it crops without a pollinator, and ripens in late summer to early autumn.

claims.text: English plum (Prunus domestica) found at Alderton, England, and introduced by the nurseryman Denyer in the 1840s as Denyer's Victoria
Pale red, lightly speckled skin over golden-yellow flesh
Versatile for both dessert and culinary use, including jam and pie fillings
Reliably self-fertile, so it crops without a pollinator
Ripens in late summer to early autumn (August to September)

sources.name: Brickfields Park: Victoria Plum (Prunus domestica Victoria)

paragraphs: Zwetschge is the German name for the prune plum (Prunus domestica), a group of dark, oblong European plums grown widely across Central Europe. The same fruit is called Quetsche in Alsace and Lorraine. Many distinct varieties exist under this name.

The fruit is small and elongated with pointed ends, deep blue to purple skin under a whitish bloom, and firm flesh that parts cleanly from the long stone (freestone). Its low water and high sugar content make it the classic plum for drying into prunes and for baking, where it holds its shape, as in the German plum cake zwetschgenkuchen.

claims.text: German name for the prune plum group of Prunus domestica, called Quetsche in Alsace and Lorraine
Oblong dark blue purple freestone fruit, low in water and high in sugar
Classic plum for drying into prunes and for baking, holding its shape
